user_input: What is this project?
retrieved_contexts:
["Section: HIGH-LEVEL ARCHITECTURE: BUILD VS SERVE
The project is split into two phases.

Build phase (run locally, occasionally): the candidate uploads documents; the system parses them, chunks them, embeds them into a vector store, and runs a trained model to estimate skill proficiency. This produces two artifacts — a ChromaDB vector index and a small JSON structured store of verified facts plus precomputed skill scores. The heavyweight models (document parsing, the skill scorer) only run here, on the candidate's own machine.

Serve phase (hosted, always on): the recruiter chat. It only reads the prebuilt artifacts and performs retrieval plus LLM calls. It deliberately avoids any heavy local model so it fits free or low-cost hosting tiers. This separation is the key design decision that makes the project cheap and simple to host.", 'Section: CANDIDATE AI AGENT — PROJECT OVERVIEW
This document describes the software project itself (its purpose, architecture, and engineering), so that a recruiter can ask questions about how the system was built. It contains no private data, no personal information, and no API keys or secrets.', "Section: STRUCTURED STORE AND VECTOR STORE
The structured store is a small JSON file holding verified profile fields plus the precomputed skill scores and their evidence. The vector store is ChromaDB, used as a persistent on-disk index with cosine similarity. Each candidate's chunks live in their own collection; this project overview lives in a separate collection of its own, queried by the search_project tool.", 'Section: WHAT THE PROJECT IS
The Candidate AI Agent is an AI-powered digital representative for a job candidate. A recruiter opens a secured, always-available chat link and asks natural-language questions about the candidate; the agent answers accurately and professionally, grounded in the candidate's own documents and a trained scoring model. It goes well beyond a standard "chat with your PDF" demo: it combines an agentic ReAct-style architecture with an advanced Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) pipeline and a purpose-built machine-learning model for skill scoring.

The whole system can run on either a 100% local open-source stack or an all-API cloud stack, selected purely through configuration. The cloud configuration is what makes it deployable as an always-on, secured service.', 'Section: SYNTHETIC TRAINING DATA GENERATION
Training the skill scorer required labeled data that does not exist publicly, so the project includes a synthetic data generation system. It builds realistic candidate personas and archetypes, generates documents with controlled skill evidence at known proficiency levels, tracks phrase usage to avoid repetition, sanitizes the output, and validates the result with statistical checks, cross-validation, and shortcut-detection (to ensure the model learns from real evidence rather than spurious cues). The generator uses async LLM calls for throughput.', "Section: AGENTIC TOOL CALLING (ReAct STYLE)
The system does not blindly query a vector database. An LLM agent reasons about each question and chooses among several tools, and it can chain multiple tools in one turn with fallbacks. The tools are:

get_structured_data: returns fixed, verified facts from a structured JSON store (full name, email, phone, links, education, years of experience, current and desired role, salary expectation, location, availability, work type, relocation).

get_skill_proficiency: returns the candidate's proficiency LEVEL (1 to 5) in a given skill, inferred by a trained scoring model from the candidate's documents, together with the supporting evidence. It can also list all assessed skills ranked by level.

search_documents: runs the full RAG pipeline over the candidate's unstructured documents for anything not covered by the other tools — projects, detailed experience, achievements, certifications.", "Section: AGENTIC TOOL CALLING (ReAct STYLE)
search_project: answers questions about THIS software project/system itself (its architecture, technology stack, features, evaluation, and how it works), using RAG over this very overview document rather than the candidate's data.

The agent chains tools intelligently. For example, if get_skill_proficiency reports that a skill was not assessed, the agent falls back to search_documents. A safety cap limits the number of tool rounds per turn to prevent loops.", 'Section: SKILL PROFICIENCY ESTIMATION — THE FLAGSHIP FEATURE
Instead of leaving "how good is the candidate at X?" to keyword matching, the project trains a dedicated NLP model to score each skill from 1 to 5 directly from the candidate's own documents. The levels mean: 1 awareness (a passing mention), 2 working familiarity, 3 competent / day-to-day use, 4 strong / leads work in it, 5 expert / authority.

At setup time, for each skill the candidate lists, the system retrieves the supporting evidence chunks from their documents and runs the trained scorer. The result — a level plus the evidence it was based on — is saved into the structured store, so the recruiter chat can later surface a verified, model-grounded level quickly instead of guessing.']
response:
The project is called the **Candidate AI Agent**, an AI-powered tool designed to act as a job candidate's digital representative. Here's a summary:

### **Purpose**  
Recruiters can interact with a secured chat interface to ask natural-language questions about a candidate's profile, skills, and experience. The agent answers accurately, drawing from the candidate's uploaded documents and a trained skill-scoring model.

### **Architecture**  
1. **Build Phase (Local/Offline):**  
   - Candidate uploads documents (CVs, certificates, etc.).  
   - The system parses, chunks, and embeds documents into a vector store (ChromaDB).  
   - A trained model estimates skill proficiency (1-5) and generates a structured JSON store of verified facts and skill scores.  

2. **Serve Phase (Hosted/Online):**  
   - A lightweight chat interface retrieves precomputed artifacts (structured store + vector index).  
   - Uses a Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) pipeline for efficient, low-cost hosting without re-running heavy models.  

### **Key Features**  
- **Skill Proficiency Scoring:** A dedicated NLP model rates skills (e.g., Python, AWS) based on document evidence, avoiding keyword-only matching.  
- **Agentic Tool Chaining:** Combines `get_structured_data`, `get_skill_proficiency`, `search_documents`, and `search_project` for dynamic, context-aware responses.  
- **Synthetic Training Data:** Generates labeled candidate data for training the skill scorer, ensuring robustness against spurious patterns.  

### **Design Philosophy**  
- Separates heavy model inference (build phase) from lightweight serving (serve phase) for cost efficiency.  
- Uses open-source or API-based stacks for flexibility (local or cloud deployment).  

This system enables recruiters to quickly assess candidates beyond surface-level data, leveraging AI to surface verified, evidence-based insights.
reference: It is an AI-powered digital representative for a job candidate: a recruiter chats with it over a secured link and it answers questions about the candidate, grounded in the candidate's own documents and a trained scoring model. It combines an agentic ReAct-style architecture with an advanced RAG pipeline.

user_input: Give me a high-level overview of the system.
retrieved_contexts: ['The Candidate AI Agent is an AI-powered tool enabling recruiters to interact with job candidates via natural language, providing accurate, document-grounded answers about their qualifications. It solves the problem of manual resume screening and fragmented candidate data by synthesizing structured profiles and skill assessments. The system uses an agentic ReAct-style architecture with a Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) pipeline, combining lightweight chat logic with precomputed artifacts from a local build phase. Its standout feature is a trained skill-proficiency scorer that estimates candidate skill levels (1–5) from uploaded documents. The tech stack includes open-source tools like ChromaDB for vector storage, JSON for structured data, and LLMs for RAG and agent reasoning, with deployment options for local or cloud hosting. Evaluation occurs during the build phase (document parsing, skill scoring) and serve phase (chat interactions), with the cloud configuration enabling secure, always-on deployment.']
response:
The Candidate AI Agent is a system designed to streamline recruiter-candidate interactions by leveraging AI to provide accurate, document-based insights into a candidate's qualifications. Here's a high-level overview:

1. **Core Functionality**:  
   - Automates resume screening and synthesizes structured candidate profiles (e.g., education, experience, skills) from uploaded documents.  
   - Uses a **Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG)** pipeline to deliver contextually grounded answers to recruiter questions.  

2. **Architecture**:  
   - **Agentic ReAct Framework**: Combines lightweight chat logic with precomputed artifacts from a local build phase.  
   - **Skill Proficiency Scoring**: A trained model estimates skill levels (1–5) for technologies/skills based on document evidence.  

3. **Tech Stack**:  
   - **Storage**: ChromaDB for vector embeddings.  
   - **Data**: JSON for structured metadata.  
   - **LLMs**: For RAG generation and agent reasoning.  

4. **Deployment**:  
   - Supports **local or cloud hosting** (e.g., secure, always-on cloud configuration).  

5. **Evaluation**:  
   - **Build Phase**: Validates document parsing, skill scoring, and RAG accuracy.  
   - **Serve Phase**: Measures chat interaction effectiveness and response quality.  

This system addresses fragmented candidate data by centralizing structured insights and skill assessments, enabling recruiters to engage with candidates via natural language queries.
reference: An agentic recruiter-facing chat that routes between verified structured facts, a trained 1-5 skill-proficiency scorer, document RAG, and project RAG. It has a build phase (local ingestion, embedding, and skill scoring) and an always-on serve phase (retrieval plus LLM calls).

user_input: What reranker does the project use?
retrieved_contexts:

user_input: How does the skill proficiency estimation work?
retrieved_contexts:
['Section: SKILL PROFICIENCY ESTIMATION — THE FLAGSHIP FEATURE
Instead of leaving "how good is the candidate at X?" to keyword matching, the project trains a dedicated NLP model to score each skill from 1 to 5 directly from the candidate's own documents. The levels mean: 1 awareness (a passing mention), 2 working familiarity, 3 competent / day-to-day use, 4 strong / leads work in it, 5 expert / authority.

At setup time, for each skill the candidate lists, the system retrieves the supporting evidence chunks from their documents and runs the trained scorer. The result — a level plus the evidence it was based on — is saved into the structured store, so the recruiter chat can later surface a verified, model-grounded level quickly instead of guessing.', "Section: AGENTIC TOOL CALLING (ReAct STYLE)
The system does not blindly query a vector database. An LLM agent reasons about each question and chooses among several tools, and it can chain multiple tools in one turn with fallbacks. The tools are:

get_structured_data: returns fixed, verified facts from a structured JSON store (full name, email, phone, links, education, years of experience, current and desired role, salary expectation, location, availability, work type, relocation).

get_skill_proficiency: returns the candidate's proficiency LEVEL (1 to 5) in a given skill, inferred by a trained scoring model from the candidate's documents, together with the supporting evidence. It can also list all assessed skills ranked by level.

search_documents: runs the full RAG pipeline over the candidate's unstructured documents for anything not covered by the other tools — projects, detailed experience, achievements, certifications.", "Section: HIGH-LEVEL ARCHITECTURE: BUILD VS SERVE
The project is split into two phases.

Build phase (run locally, occasionally): the candidate uploads documents; the system parses them, chunks them, embeds them into a vector store, and runs a trained model to estimate skill proficiency. This produces two artifacts — a ChromaDB vector index and a small JSON structured store of verified facts plus precomputed skill scores. The heavyweight models (document parsing, the skill scorer) only run here, on the candidate's own machine.

Serve phase (hosted, always on): the recruiter chat. It only reads the prebuilt artifacts and performs retrieval plus LLM calls. It deliberately avoids any heavy local model so it fits free or low-cost hosting tiers. This separation is the key design decision that makes the project cheap and simple to host.", 'Section: THE TWO USER FLOWS
Candidate Setup page: the candidate uploads supporting documents (CV, transcripts, certificates, recommendation letters) in PDF, DOCX, TXT, MD, PNG, or JPG form, fills in structured profile fields (contact details, education, experience, job preferences), lists the skills they want assessed, and triggers skill-proficiency estimation. In production this page is hidden so recruiters can never edit the profile or trigger ingestion.

Recruiter Chat page: a clean conversational interface. The recruiter types a question; the agent decides which tools to call, gathers grounded evidence, and returns a concise professional answer in the same language the recruiter used.', 'Section: SKILL PROFICIENCY ESTIMATION — THE FLAGSHIP FEATURE
The scorer is a fine-tuned DeBERTa-v3 transformer with a CORAL ordinal regression head (ordinal because the 1 to 5 levels are ordered, not arbitrary categories). The model reads the skill plus its evidence chunks, serialized in the same format it saw during training. The best configuration fine-tunes the top transformer layers; head-only and softmax-classifier variants were trained and compared. Because the scorer and the document parsing only run at setup time, they never need to be deployed to the always-on service.', "Section: AGENTIC TOOL CALLING (ReAct STYLE)
search_project: answers questions about THIS software project/system itself (its architecture, technology stack, features, evaluation, and how it works), using RAG over this very overview document rather than the candidate's data.

The agent chains tools intelligently. For example, if get_skill_proficiency reports that a skill was not assessed, the agent falls back to search_documents. A safety cap limits the number of tool rounds per turn to prevent loops.", 'Section: WHAT THE PROJECT IS
The Candidate AI Agent is an AI-powered digital representative for a job candidate. A recruiter opens a secured, always-available chat link and asks natural-language questions about the candidate; the agent answers accurately and professionally, grounded in the candidate's own documents and a trained scoring model. It goes well beyond a standard "chat with your PDF" demo: it combines an agentic ReAct-style architecture with an advanced Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) pipeline and a purpose-built machine-learning model for skill scoring.

The whole system can run on either a 100% local open-source stack or an all-API cloud stack, selected purely through configuration. The cloud configuration is what makes it deployable as an always-on, secured service.', 'Section: SYNTHETIC TRAINING DATA GENERATION
Training the skill scorer required labeled data that does not exist publicly, so the project includes a synthetic data generation system. It builds realistic candidate personas and archetypes, generates documents with controlled skill evidence at known proficiency levels, tracks phrase usage to avoid repetition, sanitizes the output, and validates the result with statistical checks, cross-validation, and shortcut-detection (to ensure the model learns from real evidence rather than spurious cues). The generator uses async LLM calls for throughput.']
response:
The skill proficiency estimation works through a dedicated NLP model trained to score skills on a 1–5 scale (1=awareness, 5=expert) based on the candidate's uploaded documents. Here's how it functions:

1. **Model & Training**:  
   A fine-tuned **DeBERTa-v3** transformer with a **CORAL ordinal regression head** (for ordered skill levels) analyzes the candidate's documents. The model is trained on synthetic data generated by the project, ensuring it learns from real evidence rather than spurious cues.

2. **Setup Phase (Build)**:  
   - Candidates upload documents (CVs, certificates, etc.) and list skills to assess.  
   - Documents are parsed, chunked, and embedded into a vector store (ChromaDB).  
   - The model evaluates each skill's proficiency by analyzing relevant evidence chunks, outputting a level (1–5) and supporting evidence.

3. **Structured Storage**:  
   Results are saved in a JSON store, linking skills to their levels and evidence. This allows recruiters to quickly access verified, model-grounded scores during chats.

4. **Fallback Mechanism**:  
   If a skill isn’t assessed, the system uses `search_documents` to find relevant context from the candidate’s files.

5. **Architecture Design**:  
   - **Build phase** (local): Runs heavy models (parsing, scoring).  
   - **Serve phase** (hosted): Lightweight, avoids re-running models, ensuring cost-efficiency.  

This approach ensures recruiters get accurate, evidence-based skill ratings without manual guesswork.
reference: A fine-tuned DeBERTa-v3 transformer with a CORAL ordinal regression head scores each listed skill from 1 to 5 using the evidence chunks retrieved from the candidate's documents. The level and its supporting evidence are saved into the structured store, and it runs only at setup time.

user_input: What evaluation frameworks does the project use?
retrieved_contexts: ['The Candidate AI Agent is an AI-powered tool enabling recruiters to interact with job candidates via natural language, providing accurate, document-grounded answers about their qualifications. It solves the problem of manual resume screening and fragmented candidate data by synthesizing structured profiles and skill assessments. The system uses an agentic ReAct-style architecture with a Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) pipeline, combining lightweight chat logic with precomputed artifacts from a local build phase. Its standout feature is a trained skill-proficiency scorer that estimates candidate skill levels (1–5) from uploaded documents. The tech stack includes open-source tools like ChromaDB for vector storage, JSON for structured data, and LLMs for RAG and agent reasoning, with deployment options for local or cloud hosting. Evaluation occurs during the build phase (document parsing, skill scoring) and serve phase (chat interactions), with the cloud configuration enabling secure, always-on deployment.']

retrieved_contexts:
['Section: THE RAG PIPELINE — RETRIEVAL
Retrieval is an advanced, multi-stage pipeline:

1. Query routing. An LLM router classifies each query as BROAD (a general overview / "who is this person" question) or SPECIFIC (any particular fact, skill, project, or detail). Broad queries are answered from the summary index; specific queries go through the full fusion path.

2. Query expansion. For specific queries, the LLM generates several query variations — a mix of short keyword-style queries (good for BM25) and natural language queries (good for vector search) — to maximize recall.

3. Hybrid fusion retrieval. The system runs both dense vector search (semantic similarity over embeddings in ChromaDB) and sparse keyword search (BM25 Okapi) for every query variation, then merges all ranked lists with Reciprocal Rank Fusion (RRF). Documents are parsed with the Unstructured library (with PyMuPDF for PDFs, python-docx for Word, and OCR via Tesseract/Pillow for images). The parser performs section-aware extraction: it detects section headers (including spaced-letter headers like "T E C H N I C A L  S K I L L S") and merges consecutive elements under the same heading. Each section is then split with a recursive character text splitter (about 1000 characters per chunk with 150 characters of overlap). Every chunk is prefixed with its section name for context and stored with provenance metadata (source file and document id) so retrieved chunks can always be traced back to their origin — which is what makes retrieval evaluation against ground-truth evidence possible.

In addition to the per-chunk index, a separate summary index is built: the LLM writes a short factual summary of each document, which is embedded and stored in its own collection. This powers the "broad" retrieval path.', "Section: HIGH-LEVEL ARCHITECTURE: BUILD VS SERVE

It deliberately avoids any heavy local model so it fits free or low-cost hosting tiers. This separation is the key design decision that makes the project cheap and simple to host."]
response: Reciprocal Rank Fusion (RRF) is used in the hybrid fusion retrieval stage of the RAG pipeline to combine results from multiple retrieval sources. It merges ranked lists from dense vector search (ChromaDB) and sparse keyword search (BM25 Okapi) by assigning equal weight to the top-ranked item (rank-0) from each list, regardless of list length or origin. This ensures diverse, high-quality candidates are retained before re-ranking, balancing semantic and keyword-based retrieval strengths.
reference: RRF merges the ranked result lists from dense vector search and BM25 keyword search (across the expanded query variations) into a single fused ranking, scoring each list independently so the top of any list gets equal weight.

Documents are ingested through a structured pipeline designed for efficient parsing, chunking, and metadata tracking. Here's how it works:

1. **Parsing**:

   - Extracts text while preserving section headers (even spaced ones like "T E C H N I C A L  S K I L L S") and merges consecutive content under the same heading.

2. **Chunking**: 
   - Splits sections into chunks using a **recursive character text splitter** (≈1000 characters per chunk with 150 overlap).
   - Each chunk is prefixed with its section name for context.

3. **Storage**: 
   - Chunks are stored with **provenance metadata** (source file and document ID) to trace them back to their origin.

4. **Build vs. Serve Phases**:
   - **Build phase** (local): Handles parsing, chunking, embedding, and skill scoring. Produces a ChromaDB vector index and structured JSON store of verified facts.
   - **Serve phase** (hosted): Uses prebuilt artifacts for retrieval, avoiding heavy models to keep hosting costs low.

This process ensures retrievability, traceability, and efficient querying of candidate documents.

user_input: What does the query router do?
retrieved_contexts: ['The Candidate AI Agent is an AI-powered tool enabling recruiters to interact with job candidates via natural language, providing accurate, document-grounded answers about their qualifications. It solves the problem of manual resume screening and fragmented candidate data by synthesizing structured profiles and skill assessments. The system uses an agentic ReAct-style architecture with a Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) pipeline, combining lightweight chat logic with precomputed artifacts from a local build phase. Its standout feature is a trained skill-proficiency scorer that estimates candidate skill levels (1–5) from uploaded documents. The tech stack includes open-source tools like ChromaDB for vector storage, JSON for structured data, and LLMs for RAG and agent reasoning, with deployment options for local or cloud hosting. Evaluation occurs during the build phase (document parsing, skill scoring) and serve phase (chat interactions), with the cloud configuration enabling secure, always-on deployment.']
response: The query router in the Candidate AI Agent system directs incoming user questions to the appropriate components of the RAG (Retrieval-Augmented Generation) pipeline. Its role is to determine whether a query should be answered using structured data (e.g., job preferences, contact info), skill proficiency scores, or document-based context retrieval. This ensures efficient and accurate responses by routing requests to the most relevant data source or model within the system's architecture.
reference: An LLM router classifies each query as BROAD (a general overview question) or SPECIFIC; broad queries are answered from the summary index, while specific queries go through the full query-expansion, hybrid-fusion, and re-ranking path.

The **build-vs-serve split** is a core design principle of the project, separating heavy processing from lightweight hosting:

### **Build Phase (Local, Occasional)**
- **Purpose**: Processes candidate documents during setup.
- **Tasks**:
  - Parses and chunks uploaded documents.
  - Embeds data into a vector store (ChromaDB).
  - Runs trained models to estimate skill proficiencies (e.g., DeBERTa-v3 scorer).
  - Produces structured outputs: a JSON store of verified facts and precomputed skill scores.
- **Models Used**: Heavyweight models (e.g., DeBERTa, Voyage AI) run here.

### **Serve Phase (Hosted, Always-On)**
- **Purpose**: Delivers the recruiter chat interface.
- **Tasks**:
  - Reads prebuilt artifacts (vector index, structured data).
  - Performs retrieval (using ChromaDB, BM25, rerankers) and LLM calls.
  - Avoids heavy local models to minimize hosting costs.
- **Models Used**: Lightweight models (e.g., Claude API, Voyage reranker) for real-time interactions.

### **Key Benefits**
- **Cost Efficiency**: Keeps hosting costs low by offloading heavy work to the build phase.
- **Scalability**: Enables deployment as a cheap, always-on cloud service (e.g., Streamlit Community Cloud).
- **Security**: Private candidate data remains isolated from public repositories.

This split allows the system to run fully locally or deploy as a secure, scalable cloud service via pluggable providers.
reference: The build phase runs locally and parses documents, embeds them, and runs the skill scorer to produce the vector index and structured store. The serve phase is hosted and always on and only performs retrieval and LLM calls, avoiding any heavy local model so it fits cheap hosting.

The training data for the skill scorer was created using a **synthetic data generation system** designed to produce labeled data where real-world datasets were unavailable. Here's how it works:

1. **Personas & Documents**: The system generates realistic candidate personas and creates controlled documents with embedded skill evidence at predefined proficiency levels (1-5).  
2. **Phrase Tracking & Sanitization**: It avoids repetitive phrasing, sanitizes outputs, and ensures data realism.  
3. **Validation**: Statistical checks, cross-validation, and shortcut-detection are used to ensure the model learns from genuine evidence, not spurious patterns.  
4. **LLM Integration**: Async LLM calls are employed to scale throughput during synthesis.  

This synthetic pipeline ensures the skill scorer is trained on diverse, high-quality data without relying on public datasets.

The **per-gate retrieval analysis** is a diagnostic feature of the evaluation suite designed to identify where relevant information is lost in the retrieval pipeline. It breaks down failures into three stages:  
1. **Ingestion**: Issues with chunk splitting, coverage, or summary quality during document processing.  
2. **Recall**: Failure to retrieve relevant chunks during the hybrid retrieval phase (dense/sparse search fusion).  
3. **Re-ranking**: Loss of relevant chunks during the final reranking step (e.g., cross-encoder reranker filtering).  

This analysis generates an HTML report with per-candidate and aggregate insights, enabling targeted improvements to the retrieval pipeline. It is part of a broader evaluation framework that also assesses tool selection, RAG quality, answer correctness, and router accuracy.
reference: For specific document questions it localizes where a relevant chunk was lost in retrieval — at ingestion, at recall (never entered the fused pool), or at re-ranking (cut from the final top-k) — instead of reporting only an aggregate recall number.

The retrieval pipeline combines the following hybrid search methods:

1. **Dense Vector Search**: Uses ChromaDB with embeddings (e.g., Voyage AI or nomic embeddings) for semantic similarity.  
2. **Sparse Keyword Search**: BM25 Okapi for keyword-based retrieval.  
3. **Hybrid Fusion**: Merges results from both methods using **Reciprocal Rank Fusion (RRF)** to balance semantic and keyword relevance.  
4. **Cross-Encoder Reranking**: Final rescored with a reranker (e.g., Voyage rerank or Qwen3 Reranker) to prioritize the most relevant chunks.  

This approach ensures robustness by leveraging both semantic and keyword-based retrieval, with fusion and reranking to optimize recall and precision.
reference: Dense vector search over ChromaDB embeddings and sparse BM25 Okapi keyword search, fused with Reciprocal Rank Fusion and then re-ranked by a cross-encoder.

Reranker. The Qwen3 reranker is a causal language model scored, per its model card, by reading the probability of the "yes" token over a query/document relevance prompt — not a naive classification head. A "none" option skips reranking.', "Section: HIGH-LEVEL ARCHITECTURE: BUILD VS SERVE
The project is split into two phases.

Build phase (run locally, occasionally): the candidate uploads documents; the system parses them, chunks them, embeds them into a vector store, and runs a trained model to estimate skill proficiency. This produces two artifacts — a ChromaDB vector index and a small JSON structured store of verified facts plus precomputed skill scores. The heavyweight models (document parsing, the skill scorer) only run here, on the candidate's own machine.

Serve phase (hosted, always on): the recruiter chat. It only reads the prebuilt artifacts and performs retrieval plus LLM calls. It deliberately avoids any heavy local model so it fits free or low-cost hosting tiers. This separation is the key design decision that makes the project cheap and simple to host.", "Section: THE EVALUATION SUITE

search_project: answers questions about THIS software project/system itself (its architecture, technology stack, features, evaluation, and how it works), using RAG over this very overview document rather than the candidate's data.

The agent chains tools intelligently. For example, if get_skill_proficiency reports that a skill was not assessed, the agent falls back to search_documents. A safety cap limits the number of tool rounds per turn to prevent loops.", 'Section: SYNTHETIC TRAINING DATA GENERATION
Training the skill scorer required labeled data that does not exist publicly, so the project includes a synthetic data generation system. It builds realistic candidate personas and archetypes, generates documents with controlled skill evidence at known proficiency levels, tracks phrase usage to avoid repetition, sanitizes the output, and validates the result with statistical checks, cross-validation, and shortcut-detection (to ensure the model learns from real evidence rather than spurious cues). The generator uses async LLM calls for throughput.', 'Section: WHAT THE PROJECT IS
The Candidate AI Agent is an AI-powered digital representative for a job candidate. A recruiter opens a secured, always-available chat link and asks natural-language questions about the candidate; the agent answers accurately and professionally, grounded in the candidate's own documents and a trained scoring model. It goes well beyond a standard "chat with your PDF" demo: it combines an agentic ReAct-style architecture with an advanced Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) pipeline and a purpose-built machine-learning model for skill scoring.

The whole system can run on either a 100% local open-source stack or an all-API cloud stack, selected purely through configuration. The cloud configuration is what makes it deployable as an always-on, secured service.', "Section: DEPLOYMENT AND SECURITY
The recruiter chat deploys to a Streamlit-based host (such as Streamlit Community Cloud or Hugging Face Spaces) over HTTPS. Access is gated by a single shared access code suited to a single-candidate app. In production only the recruiter chat is served; the setup page is hidden. The candidate's private data is kept out of any public repository. Running costs are minimal: the all-API serve path uses a fast, inexpensive Claude model for the agent and a low-cost Voyage tier for embeddings and reranking, typically only a few dollars per month, and the hosting itself can be free."]
response: The project does use prompt caching. According to the documentation, prompt caching is applied to the frozen system prompt and tool definitions in the LLM layer to reduce costs, particularly when using the Anthropic Claude API or local Ollama models. This optimization helps minimize redundant computations during the agent loop and query processing.
